questions.prompt: According to the lesson, what is the difference between a topic and a main argument?

options.text: A topic is what the text is about; a main argument is what the writer claims about it.
A topic is always longer than a main argument.
A main argument only appears in the conclusion.
A topic and a main argument are the same thing.

questions.explanation: The lesson distinguishes topic (the subject matter) from main argument (the writer's central claim about that subject). The main argument is usually stated early and restated at the end, not only in the conclusion — and length has nothing to do with the difference.

questions.prompt: A writer uses the phrase "so-called experts" when describing people who support a policy. What tone does this suggest?

options.text: Enthusiastic
Ironic or sceptical
Cautious and balanced
Neutral and objective

questions.explanation: "So-called" signals that the writer is questioning whether those people really deserve the label "experts" — this is ironic or sceptical language. Enthusiastic writers use words like "remarkable", balanced writers use "while… nevertheless", and neutral writers avoid evaluative phrases like "so-called".

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of an INFERENCE question?

options.text: "According to the text, how many people lost their jobs?"
"What does the word 'consternation' mean in line 5?"
"What does the writer suggest about the reliability of official figures?"
"In which paragraph does the writer mention the government?"

questions.explanation: "What does the writer suggest" is the classic phrasing for an inference question — the answer is implied, not directly stated. "According to the text" (a) asks for directly stated information, "what does the word mean" (b) is vocabulary in context, and "in which paragraph" (d) is a locating task.

questions.prompt:
Read: "Sales fell by 12% in Q3. This proves that the government's tax policy has been a catastrophic failure."
Which part is a FACT?

options.text: The government's tax policy has been a catastrophic failure.
Both statements are facts.
Neither statement is a fact.
Sales fell by 12% in Q3.

questions.explanation: "Sales fell by 12% in Q3" is a verifiable statistic — a fact. "The government's tax policy has been a catastrophic failure" is a judgment drawn from that statistic — an opinion. The lesson specifically warns that a statistic is a fact but a conclusion drawn from it is often an opinion.

questions.prompt: According to the B2 reading workflow in the lesson, what should you do FIRST when you open a reading text?

options.text: Read every word carefully from start to finish.
Look up unknown vocabulary.
Skim the whole text to get the topic, structure, and tone.
Answer the easiest question first, then read the text.

questions.explanation: The five-step workflow starts with skimming the whole text (about 30 seconds) to get the topic, structure, and tone before doing anything else. Reading every word carefully comes later when you re-read relevant sections; looking up vocabulary is not part of exam strategy; and answering without reading is the opposite of the workflow.
